ElectraWoman & DynaGirl must reach the Secret Scientific Formula space in their ElectraCar and return to ElectraBase while using different combinations of Computer Power symbol cards to thwart the bad guys:  The Sorcerer, The Pharaoh, The Empress of Evil, The Spider Lady, Glitter Rock, and Ali Baba.

Each Trouble Space depicts a different combination of Computer Power symbols needed in order to overcome it.  Computer Powers include ElectraData, ElectraForce, ElectraBeam, and a few Super Power cards (a combination of all three).  If a player does not have the right combination of Computer Powers for that particular Trouble Space, they must remain in it until the correct combination is achieved.  A card is drawn every turn, regardless of whether a player can move.

Additional cards used to obstruct opposing players include ElectraPush and ElectraComp Blocked.

This game is based on the short-lived Sid and Marty Krofft live action children’s television series from 1976, starring Deidre Hall in her first major television role as ElectraWoman and Judy Strangis as DynaGirl.

Players seek to answer trivia regarding famous news stories dating back as early as 1900.  Categories include: 20th Century, 21st Century, Sports, Arts & Entertainment, and Science & Technology.

Victory is achieved by scoring five of one category, a 3/2 combination, or a run including each category, so players are awarded both for breadth of knowledge as well as expertise.

Of particular interest to trivia buffs is that each question, when answered, includes the story behind the headline.

"The game of strategy, fun, and fascinating facts."

www.newshoundgame.com

April 1863
Union General Ulysses S. Grant embarks on another attempt to wrest control of the last major Confederate stronghold on the Mississippi River from the rebels; the fortress city of Vicksburg. Numerous other attempts have failed miserably. The ill-fated campaign from the north, short circuited by that devil Forrest's raid on Grant's supply lines. The jinxed canal, dug through the swamps in an attempt to bypass the city's guns. Now another plan is in motion. Grant will march his Army of the Tennessee through the Louisiana swamps, sneak his ships past the rebel fortress in a daring run of the gauntlet, conduct the largest amphibious invasion in U.S. history, separate himself from his supply base, wedge himself in between two rebel armies, and seize control of the city from the east! All the while battling politicians and public opinion as much as the Confederate armies. Somehow, his daring plan worked, and the Mississippi once again flowed unvexed to the sea.

But things could have been different. Find out how....

This operational level game uses the block unit system to re-create the limited intelligence of the actual campaign. Players maneuver their forces without adequate knowledge of their opponents strengths or even location. The game board spans the campaign topography, from the swamps of eastern Louisiana to the mountainous terrain around Jackson Mississippi. A point to point movement system is employed on a 20 x 24 playing map. Limited command abilities and critical supply wagons are key to managing your armies and achieving victory.

Kruzno is an abstract strategy game usually played with two players on a hexagonally tiled board. Though the rules are extremely simple (three minutes to learn for most players), the number of possible moves and interactions are comparable to chess.

Kruzno uses standard chess pieces (bishops, knights and rooks) but is more closely related to hexagonal checkers and R.C. Bell's Jungle Game. Like the Jungle Game, capturing is non-transitive: bishops capture knights; knights capture rooks; rooks capture bishops. This split between offense and defense means that most standard strategies of move and capture have to be scrapped or reworked.

Game variants include versions with reduced number of pieces for faster play and expansion sets for three players.

Home Page: http://www.kruzno.com/
Card driven movement game for children. Based on the "Peepul Pals" line of books and dolls published by Whitman in the 1960's

Played on a large (approx 4 foot x 4 foot) vinyl mat.  Players try to blow plastic balls across another player's "loser line". You must try to prevent other player's ball from crossing your line or you earn 1 loser card.  Be careful if you touch your ball you earn 2 loser cards.

The game ends when the 45 loser cards are given out.  The player with the fewest cards wins.
Spin and move children's game.  Featuring the cats Jean-Tom and Mewsette traveling through Paris.
Players race around the State Fair ground stopping at attractions to collect prizes.  Be the first player to collect 6 different prizes and you win.
Spin and Move boat racing game.

Gulf War is a Malaysian board game from 1990, using as its theme the then ongoing war in the Persian gulf. Its game mechanism is borrowed from An Qi (literally dark chess, because pieces are covered at the start and so unknown to the players: it is a game played using Xiangqi pieces). Each side has a number of pieces ranking from President down to Hostages or Civilians, and also Bombs, Fighter Planes and Oil-fields. Like An Qi (and for that matter games like LuZhanJunQi and Stratego), the pieces have a ranked order and higher ranked pieces eliminate lower ranked ones. Pieces start face-down and are randomly distributed in alternate colors on the 6X6 square grid that is the game board. Players take turn to expose pieces and once exposed, pieces may be moved one space orthogonally. Enemy pieces are eliminated by a higher or equal ranked piece moving on to them. There are special rules for specific pieces - the Assassin may eliminate the President, Bombs neutralize (i.e. both pieces get eliminated) all other pieces, capturing an Oil-field/Hostages/Civilians piece allows substitution of your own piece for a previously eliminated piece, etc. The Fighter Plane has the capture of the Pao (Cannon) in An Qi - just like in Xiangqi, it jumps over another piece to land on a distal piece and capture it. 
The game is won by the player who is able to capture both the opposing President and Vice-president.

The game has interesting details - one player takes the Iraqi side and the other is the Allied side. The President, Vice-president and Ally pieces are different and have pictures appropriate to the side (Saddam, Bush, Gaddfi and Thatcher all feature). Another difference is that the Iraqis have a Civilians piece but the Allied forces has a Hostages piece instead (in the game, they are equivalent). The board comes with two sides - one side has yellow and blue circles to show where the pieces go. The other has the same 6X6 grid superimposed on a background map showing the Persian gulf.

Spin and move children's game.  Based on the TV show Bozo the Clown.  The board shows Bozo in the Circus,under the big top.

Climb the ladder, go across the high wire, if you fall there is a safety net, as you race to the finish line.

World Tank Campaigns (WTC) is a series of tactical games that employ cards, miniatures, and boards in a fast-playing action game designed primarly for solitaire, with one-on-one action as well. WTC is the brainchild of Dana Lombardy, designer of the award winning Streets of Stalingrad.

The game is card driven with dice used for combat and special actions.  Combat is represented on a tactical to operational level.

World Tank Campaigns: Bulge: Germany’s last gamble in the Ardennes to change the course of the war initially surprised the American forces, but it resulted in a major Allied victory. Each player play the role of a German or American tank commander directing a platoon of tanks with supporting forces.  The game plays out on a Battle Board unique to the scenario and tanks used in that scenario.

The World Tank Campaigns: Bulge Set has everything needed to play SOLITAIRE and 2-PLAYER games:


     2 historically accurate, incredibly detailed 1/144 scale plastic tanks
     Miniature tanks are pre-painted and fully assembled and include a historical information sheet about each tank
     2 full-color battle “boards” unique to the Ardennes and the Sherman M4A3 and Panther G tanks
     6 custom designed army-specic dice, plus standard d6 & d10
     88 full-color playing cards make gameplay fast and exciting
     55 die-cut play aid tokens (not show here), illustrated rulebook, and Quick Start Rules for the solitaire game


2 EXPANSION Sets include:


     22 additional cards, a Jagdpanzer IV tank-killer or King Tiger super heavy tank with their own unique battle boards and scenarios.


Home Page of the Game: http://www.world-tank-campaigns.com/
Home Page for the Miniatures: http://www.world-tank-museum.com/

A football board game that is designed to be played while watching actual games on TV.  Players place bets on the board indicating what play they think the offensive or defensive will make in the actual game.

In Hand 2 Hand, players take turns drawing various things while other players attempt to guess what's being drawn.  Points are scored for correct guesses.

The trick is that partners must use the "double-handed drawing arm", working together to properly draw each subject.

Money Maker, A game of free enterprise.  Is a very monopoly like game that is used to market local businesses.  It consists of a gameboard, 2 dice, 4 pawns, a rules booklet, money in four denominations, a pad of Business Ownership and Expansion Record sheets, and a set of risk cards.  Rather than using houses and such to show ownership and improvement (as monopoly does) Branches are noted on the sheets.  On the gameboard all of the businesses are representations of local businesses.

Designed to easily control 15-20 capital ships a side (which can be 4-5 squadrons), while concentrating on squadron level tactics, Firepower Pass uses a unique command oriented system that allows units to attack as individual ships (i.e. at a tactical level) yet defend and maneuver in 3D as squadrons. Octagonal 3D movement tiles easily and elegantly depict the 2D vector of 3D movement.
This is a memory game where each player is trying to find and build the alphabet made of puzzle pieces of their chosen color.

description:
A themed Chutes & Ladders variant with some additions, like Rescue Pack squares that allow an extra roll, hang glider squares that allow (and force) the player to switch positions with someone else.  The board now goes to 91 and there's a dinosaur track on top.

Alternate cooperative rules are included with some interesting additional developments.

Includes board, rules, 6 pawns, 1 die, rubber dinosaur.
Board game based on Horse Show Jumping. The game has four plastic horses with riders, a variety of fences, walls and other items to make up the Show Jumping course on the board.

Hell & Uncivil Disorder is a set of miniature wargame rules for Brushfire Wars and "Magic" in the 20th Century (there is also a Science Fiction option because Jim Webster is "happily confident our descendants will be as incompetent as we are").
The rules are suitable for use with a variety of figure scales (including a 1/300 variant) with a ground scale of approximately 20 yards to the inch.
The rules are intended for conflicts where first world forces are fighting third world forces and hence treats technology as magic (as per the well know Arthur C Clarke quotation) but may be used for any modern conflict.

Imperial Commander is a set of Tactical level miniature wargame rules for Science-Fiction combat.  It was based around the Tabletop Figures range of 15mm Sci-Fi figures and was designed to give a fast game when using around 50 figures per side.
The rules cover light infantry through to power armor, force shields and vehicles.  They also contain some army lists and conversions from Laserburn and Combat 3000.

description:

A game very much in the vein of Trivial Pursuit. Semantic requires players to answer questions based on the meaning of words. Players must complete a path around the board answering questions as they go. Movement is based on a dice roll.

This game from the Wing National Game Company of St. Augustine, FLA was indeed copyrighted in 1898.

[The box says this game was published by Parker Brothers.]

The game consists of a Battle Ground with 31 Navy Paths (31 circles) each of which are intersected at 5 or 6 places by other paths. These intersections are called fighting positions.  Red, White and Blue designs across the battleground are called Line of Shot.

The game comes with 9 white and 9 black washers that represent Battleships from two Navies.

Each navy changes form (moves) in turn. Only one move is allowed at a time. The sinking of an opponents ship occurs when you manage to get two of your ships on the same line of shot as that of an enemy ship, with your nearest ship half way between the enemy and your back ship. When the range is so short that your two ships and the enemy lay in 3 consecutive positions then the enemy is captured and not sunk. When that happens you may remove the captured ship and place any of your ships in one of the starting positions. This allows you to fix placement errors by returning your ship to the start.

Free translation from back of the box:

Do you dream about becoming a successful show jumping star just like Malin Baryard? In Malin’s own game WORLD CUP you get to test your talent in exciting jumping competitions. Initially you compete on a borrowed horse and try to collect enough prices to get all necessary equipment and a horse of your own. With that accomplished you just have to win one final competition to become the World Cup champion. All you need is luck, a bit of tactics and some horse knowledge.

Event cards and trivia questions make the game full of action and lots of fun. You also get to ride different horses and the better you perform the better horses you get to ride. Many of the available horses are "celebrities" and if you are really good you might even get to jump Butterfly Flip!

Good luck!

Malin Baryard

It´s a little like the German game "Mau Maus" resp. "UNO".
There are four colors with 18 cards, of 5 types including the law cards. Also there jokers.
You start the game with 12 coins and every round with 6 hand cards. The game ends when one player has no more money left at the end of a round. The player with the most money wins.
On your turn you place a card on the discard pile. It has to match the color or the type of the last card. If you can´t place a card you have to draw a new one. If you can´t place it either your turn ends.
The round ends when one player has no more cards. Leftover cards cost you money and round end law cards are applied.
The law-cards help you or force other players to do silly things every turn or get a disadvantage during the round or at the end of it.
Some of the cards have instant effects on them.

After a very long night of fishing the boats replete with fish return to the port. In the Strap begins the auction of all the apprehensions, every player is interested in obtaining the highest price, and of all those more species better, spending the less amount of money.

In Top Aktionär (Top Stockholder) the goal is to become the richest player in the defined play-time.  To achieve this goal, players buy and sell stock.    Whoever buys stock, gets shares and then must pay dividends.  In the game,it is possible to purchase many stocks, and to unexpectedly be tossed out of control by other players.


Bei Top Aktionär geht es darum, nach Ablauf der festgesetzten Spielzeit der reichste Spieler zu sein.
Zu diesem Zweck kauft und verkauft man Aktien. Wer Aktien besitzt, erzielt Einnahmen und muss auch Dividenden zahlen.
Im Spiel geht es darum, möglichst viele Aktien zu erwerben, aber auch immer für unerwartete Ausgabengerüstet zu sein.

Be the king of Pot, Hash and 'Shrooms.  You roll dice and progress around the board.  In the process you 'make connections' wherein you can buy and sell illegal commodities.

boardgame: The Bee Safe or Bee Sorry Game
description:
The Bee Safe or Be Sorry Game helps kids be aware of dangers. It features "Stinger" and "Buzzie".

The age recommendation is 4 and up.

The players are represented by playing pieces which are shapes, a square, a circle, a triangle, and a rectangle.  There are Honeycomb cards, Stinging Bee cards, Safety First cards, Buzzie Bee cards, Safehouse cards, Stranger Cards, and a spinner.

Players start with three Honeycomb cards and move about the board drawing the other cards.  The other cards have situations on them that are to be read and then directions to either move (forwards or backwards) and/or draw/lose Honeycomb cards.  The situations all have to do with safety.

Th winner is the first to reach the hive first or obtain 15 Honeycomb cards!

description: This is a trivia game based on Yellowstone Park - the object to collect unique sets of park "treasures (which are cards of photos for example).  Each trivia question has 3 level of difficulties.

This game is a very simple and typical roll and move game, where players try to go from the beginning to the track which has 100 positions. In places where there's a red position instead of a yellow, players encounter different kinds of problems which may affect who's the winner.

Paletti game no. 2893

In this game, the board is built of puzzle pieces at the beginning of the game. Each puzzle piece contains a question category. All the players use the same button, which is moved according to a die throw. The question is asked in the category of where the button gets to. There is also an extra category, which contains yes-no questions of all the categories.

This game seems to be a Finnish version of the game called "Geni!" (http://www.boardgamegeek.com/game/22289). This is written in the box cover too. However, there are some differences. For example, in the Finnish version there are 56 puzzle pieces instead of 15 in Geni!

Great on the road, at home, and every place in between. Travel fun for the whole family.

Game, Set & Match is a Tennis board game that uses a die and deck of cards for playing shots, and a board and pawn to show the position of the ball on court.
Players serve by rolling a die marked Ace/Let/In/Out, then if the ball is in play, cards are played for each shot of the rally that determine the stroke played and the position of the ball on the court. Singles or doubles can be played.

The Player's Guide contains articles that describe tips, tactics, strategies, and variants available for Paths of Glory. In addition to the 48-page magazine, the Player's Guide includes 20 new cards and a 1/4 sheet of additional counters.

Recent deluxe editions of the base game (6th edition 2018 and later) already contain the "optional counters first released in the POG Player’s Guide in 2002" and the "20 Optional Cards from the POG Player’s Guide".


(from GMT Games's website:)

Features
The Paths of Glory Player's Guide is designed as an expansion kit for your enjoyment of this wonderful game, with exciting and informative articles featuring strategies for you to employ, new scenarios and variants, new playing cards, in-depth analysis of key features in the game, and so much more. Executive Editor Wray Ferrell has put together an excellent team of authors and Managing Editor Stuart K. Tucker's handsome layout design makes reading and utilizing the Player's Guide a pleasure.

Contents and Authors

Strategy

    The Opening Guns (Peter P. Perla) - Learn the Giuco Sedano, Warsaw Waltz and Tarnopol Tango in this article that covers a myriad of the many ways to experience August 1914 in Paths of Glory.
    Goin' Mobile (Kris Weinschneker) - Play of the Mobilization deck involves some critical choices about War Status and Reinforcements.
    Limited War Cards (Kris Weinschneker) - Continuing his discussion of the card decks, Kris offers his suggestions on getting from "Limited" to "Total" war quickly.
    Total War Cards (Kris Weinschneker) - Wrapping up his review of the cards, Kris offers some endgame card play suggestions.


New Scenarios and Cards

    New Scenarios (Ted Raicer) - The game's designer provides the official starting setups, including card dispositions, for games begun in 1915, 1916, or 1917.
    Adding More Paths to Your Deck (Ted Raicer) - Twenty new cards are included as an insert to this magazine. Here are their historical bases.


Analysis

    PoG Supply (Ananda Gupta) - The disruption of supply lines can cause the collapse of a front. Each theater has a number of key locations in the defense of those vital supply lines.
    Parade of Fools: The Italian Front (Ananda Gupta) - The Italian front offers both promise and danger to both sides, as they attempt to overcome the foibles of their "weak sister" armies.
    Optional Rules: Assessing the Balance (Ananda Gupta) - How effective are the mainstream optional rules at establishing a balanced game?


Cybergaming

    PBEM Primer (Kris Weinschneker) - A primer on playing Paths of Glory via Play By Email.


Variants

    Variant Cards (Tod Reiser) - More card ideas to consider adding to your deck.
    Pre-War Options (Ted Raicer) - For those wanting to vary the starting force setup, here are some ideas for each major nation.
    Strategic Options (Rick D. Stuart) - Rick provides us with more options to alter game play - some affecting the setup, some used later in the game.
    Historical Variants (Brad Martin) - For those seeking to experiment with history and game balance, here are more variant rule ideas.

From the Publisher´s web
The Harpoon Player's Handbook is a 28 page compilation of all the charts and tables contained in the rulebook. Use of this playing aid greatly assists the flow of play in multiple player games, as all of the data is right at hand, along with references to the appropriate section of the rulebook.


    The Harpoon Series


Expands

    Harpoon 4

description: A solo game that enables players to play a complete Test Match in around two hours. You can add individual stats for players, but it adds around 25% to the time to play. A quick, easy and very flexible and exciting game. Perfect for cricket fans who want to play entire series without it becoming a life's work.

This game is from 1900 and was published by the J. Ottmann Litho Company of New York.

The directions say that Commerce is "the most amusing game on the market"

The game is comprised of a deck of cards with 8 suits
 (Pork, Sugar, Cotton, Corn, Coffee, Oats, Flour and Butter) of 6 cards in each suit, making 48 cards in all.

After shuffling the cards, deal each player 6 cards. Each player sorts the cards that they have and then decides which suit they will try to corner.

The object of the game is to obtain all of the cards of one suit by trading with other players.

To play you take from your hand a card or cards of similar suite and holding them up call "Trade 2 or trade 3. All players do that at the same time so there is lots of yelling going on. Sorta like what happens on the New York Stock Exchange. People make trades of cards. So you could trade 2 with someone else then find that the 2 that you receive match a card or two that you already have in your hand and now you might try and trade 3 for 3. When the first player succeeds in getting all of the cards in a suit the player calls "corner" shows the cards to everyone else. First player to get 3  corners wins!

The game is played on a hexagon board composed of 6 blue triangle fields known as Playing Fields. The Playing Fields are divided by rows of red playing spots running toward the center of the board known as Alleys. These Alleys may be traveled from one end to another in a single move if desired. In each game there are 30 shells divided into groups of 5, each group has a different  color. There are also 6 counters numbered 5,10,15,20,25 and 50. At the beginning each player draws a counter, looks at it and then places it beneath one of his shells so that no opponent knows under which shell the counter is.  . The object of the game is to move a shell into the SHELL OUT area of an opponent. You may attempt to capture a shell of your opponent. If you are next to a opponents shell you simply jump on it. Most shells have nothing under them and simply result in your losing your shell. If you find the shell with the scoring marker under it you score it. The hand is over when someone moves a loaded shell unto the opponents SHELL OUT area.

Rat Race is a card game that is played as fast as possible, all players playing cards simultaneously.  Each player has a cheese card in a different maze material (stone, brick, wood, and metal). Swedish card game featuring Alfons and friends from the books and short films by Gunilla Bergström. 58 cards are dealt equally among the players. One card is placed face-up on the table. The starting player turns up the top card from his deck. If it is the same character as the open card, or if the balloons on the cards are the same color, the player plays the card on a common pile and continues turning his cards. When the cards don´t match the player discards his card and the next player turns up his top card. If a player turns up a card that is both the same character and the same balloon color as the open card, he may put all his discards on the pile. Players may have to turn their discard pile into a new draw deck. The first player to run out of cards is the winner.

No decisions to make at all, but an easy game for small kids.
Early 20th century roll and move Bicycle race game.  The colorful board represents a 100 mile race. Published in Australia.

A very different type of roll and move game.

The board has been replaced by two rolled paper bands in plastic boxes.  The Bikes with riders sit on top.

Dice are rolled to determine how far your rider goes, but instead of moving the rider across a board, you simply turn the rollers to advance the roll (which serves as the tracks).

Nitty Gritty: The Star Game

If you are an eager beaver, here's your chance to get even steven with any funny bunny, providing you don't get into double trouble in which case any hanky panky could become hairy scary.  So don't dilly dally, but get down to the nitty gritty so you can razzle dazzle without phoney baloney - and that, silly Billy, is super duper.  Does this sound like mumbo jumbo?  (From the box)

The game consists of 154 printed cards which, when properly matched with each other, make 77 pairs.  Pair examples are: Mellow Yellow / Roly Poly / Plain Jane / Hebbie Jebbies...etc.

Each of the 154 cards has one word and from one to three stars printed on the face of the card.  The number of stars indicates the value of the card.

The purpose of the game is to win as many matched pairs of cards as possible.  When the stockpile of cards is gone, the player with the greatest number of stars is the winner.
Roll and move bicycle race game set in the Alps. Austrian publisher.

A scenario pack for Critical Hit's Advance Tobruk System (ATS).

The package contains two maps and 6 scenarios all covering actions on the Western Front in 1944.

Note that no rules and counters are provided so that ownership of other games in this system (e.g. Scottish Corridor) and/or spare countersheets obtainable from Critical Hit is required to play.

Expands

    Advanced Tobruk: Tank Battles in North Africa 1940-43
    Scottish Corridor: Lion Rampant

Bicycle race game from Mattel Italy.  Features dice that include results with fatigue, punctures and energy.

Has 2 2-sided boards for 4 different race courses

From publisher's website:

After the death of the beloved Royal Mage of the kingdom, the king of the land set out a challenge to find his replacement. Wizards from far and wide came to vie for the position. As the challenge was set forth a few of the smarter and sneakier ones thought of a good way to get a leg up. They made their way into the secret dungeon of the former Royal Mage and decided to loot it for what they needed to win... But only one can win, so the battle for the loot began.

Lootin' Wizards is a fun group card/board game where you race around the randomly placed dungeon, trying to get more loot than your opponents. Make spells to battle each other, using the ingenious three card spell system (for thousands of possible creations). Screw up their spells with troublesome Side Effects. Foil them with entertaining Trap cards. Use helpful Items to aid your quest to grab all the Loot you can.

http://www.dragavangames.com/

Conga is a two-dimensional game which combines characteristics of Kalah games with four in a row. One players uses 10 black seeds, the other 10 white seeds. Players start with their cache of seeds in opposite corners of the board. The contents of a pit are distributed into the succeeding pits in a particular manner not unlike the "sowing" that happens in Mancala games. The object of the game is to form a row of four occupied holes (the colors of the seeds don't matter), diagonally or orthogonally. In addition, you win if your opponent can't move.

Play doctor and be the first to make the teddy bear better.  Move your character around the board, placing bandages on boo-boos as you go.  Watch out for gauzy shortcuts.  When you reach the lollipop, flip it over and put it on the bears mouth.  You made him smile and you win.

Combination Board, 4 Games (boxed):
Manufactured by Wilder Mfg. Co., St. Louis, MO., U.S.A., around 1920, before they carved up Germany and Austria-Hungary and created Poland, etc., because the map matches 1918 Europe.
This set of 4 games (designed for 2 to 4 players) in one box includes two folding game boards with a game on each side. The four games are: 1. Indian Home Game (looks and plays like Parcheesi); 2. Game Hunt (move around the board scoring points for shooting critters -- Rabbit = 1, Lion = 12); 3. Army and Navy (move around a map of WWI Europe, scoring points for capturing enemy officers and weapons -- Zeppelin = 1, Flag and General = 10); 4. Auto Race (race around a track, avoid obstacles, cross finish line 1st).

Contents: Two 12" x 12" mounted game boards with a different game on each side, 16 1/2-inch round wooden playing pieces (4 red, 4 yellow, 4 green, 4 blue), and 1D6 (wooden) in a 6.5" x 14" x 1.5" flat box. The rules for 3 games are inside the lid; the Auto Race game has the rules right on the board.

A game in Kärnan´s line of wooden games for children. This is an educational game to help children learn to read and write. The game features 77 wooden tiles with letters and 8 double-sided boards with pictures and some letters. Players connect tiles to make the words matching the picture on each line.

5 years and up.

Rumble Robots was a children's collectible card game sold in starter decks and booster packs circa 2001.  The premise is based around warring factions of sci-fi robots.  There are two sides: a blue team of good guys, and a red team of bad guys, each lead by a robot king.  The blue king is King El Smasho and the red king is King Slugnut.  The cards themselves are called Power Cards and contain bar codes for interaction with Rumble Robots toys.  Each card has a "power level" from 1-5 which corresponds to both its rarity and relative usefulness in the game.  In addition, there are "platinum" cards, more powerful than level 5 cards.  One expansion set was released, "Rumble Robots: Invasion", which focused on the two king characters as well as adding a further rarity level called "superpower" cards.
All players must hold hands to make Tommy the telephone talk.  Tommy tells you what picture to find by making silly sounds.  When Tommy says Time's Up, the person with the most discs wins.

The Snoopy Card Game board consists of eight popular Peanuts characters--Charlie Brown, Sally, Lucy, Schroeder, Peppermint Patty, Woodstock, Franklin and Linus. Players try to march along the paw-print path to the finish by matching cards from their hands with the characters on the board.  By collecting "scoring bowls" players move one, two, three or four spaces at a time.  Each round ends with players drawing a card from the player to their right.  Amongst the various character cards are eight "Snoopy Ha-Ha" cards which serve as dead spots in your hand since none of them can match the characters on the board.  First player to reach the finish is the winner.
Party game where players act out words and their teammates try to guess the word. Basically it´s charades, but you may make sounds and whistle tunes as long as you don´t use actual words.

Each player tries to match the color and shape inside the egg with the ones on their cards.  The first player to match all their cards to the eggs wins.

Ages 3-7.

This game is a memory-variant with pictures of the city or region the game is developed for. There are many different editions for about 200 cities or regions in Germany.

Each pair of tiles has a set of corresponding questions on it, where each tiles also gives the answer to the other one.
This way, several trivia-games can be played. Two special dice are included.

The rules describe 7 different way of plays.

An edition of the commercially successful board game, Monopoly. This edition takes in the English town of Reading. Popular and well known landmarks are for sale including the Madejski Stadium, the Abbey Ruins and The Oracle shopping centre. Local companies and organisations logos feature prominently on the gameboard.

At first sight this "card tennis" game resembles the game Tennis ( http://boardgamegeek.com/game/2841 ) a lot. On close examination, however, there are differences which will be discussed underneath.
First of all it should be noted that the (c) from this game is 1973: two years before Tennis was released! Further, on the box it says (literal translation) "The first and only cardgame on the world based on tennis". The box furthermore states that  the game was issued on occasion of the 75th year of the Dutch Tennis Association. However, a publisher cannot be found on box, or on rule sheets. Based on these data I do believe that this was the predecessor of Tennis.
Differences with Tennis: a) In this game there is no 'ball' nor a pawn (you just move the stack of played cards to the correct position). b) Cards are strictly for either backhand or forehand strokes (indicated with either B or F). c) There are more rules of which card may be played on what card (eg. smash may only following a lob).

Be the first to collect all the treasures and escape from the dungeon.

Dungeon Escape is a roll and move, dungeon crawl themed game for children ages 7 and up. Move your two children through the maze while avoiding the guards, who will send a player's piece back to the keep. Before a player's piece can exit the dungeon it must land on each of the four marked treasure spaces - Sword, Jewel, Book of Spells, and Key - and collect the corresponding treasures.

The game takes its theme from the formation of the Saxon Shieldwall in an abstract form. The pieces reflect this being resin cast representations of stylized Norman and Saxon Infantry.
  The objective of Shieldwall is to capture positions to the rear of the opponent's flanks. Offensive pieces can only attack in pairs and the defending pieces can be formed into stronger units called Shieldwalls.
 The Designer claims that the rules are easy to learn yet the depth of strategy belies its apparent simplicity.

boardgame: The Game of the Wizard of Oz
description:
A roll and move game in which players move counters from different initial positions: Dorothy starts in Kansas, the other players in positions ahead of her (Scarecrow, Tin Man, and Lion, in that order.)  The point of the game is to reach the Witch's Castle and then return to the Emerald City.

Similar in design and mechanics to the game Kentucky Derby (1938), and of typical quality of other Whitman Publishing games (paper boards, flimsy boxes, and punch-out paper pawns of other games) it looks as if Whitman didn't spend a lot on the construction of their games, apparently blowing all their budget on the purchase of the character license.

The game contains:

Linen paper game board 
1 dice
4 wooden markers
Two part box with color pictorial cover (measuring 7"x13"x1")

The featured art, though crude, is apparently inspired by the MGM Wizard Of Oz movie.

Look out...here comes the runaway clock!

Players must arrange the tiles and create a "track" for the oncoming clock before the alarm goes off and it's too late!

The clock has speed levels for players who dare to rev up the frantic fun in this family game of split second action.

A cross between a sliding 15 puzzle and a pipeline game.

boardgame: Robber Barons of the Gilded Age
description: Some superficial similarities to Monopoly such as Board shape and pawn movement. Players build up Wealth and Economic Resources. However to win, you have to have the best Legacy, which means you must convert some of that wealth into philanthropy before the game ends. Monopolies are based on 20 different Industries including Oil, Banking, Railroads, Steel, etc.  Players can own holdings within the same space. Not complex but it does offer lots of period flavor. A complete copy of the rules are available for free at: http://www.angelfire.com/games2/warpspawn/RBGA.html

Donner Knispel is very much like Pachisi, but instead of dice rolling, movement is done by playing cards. Card have values and special abilities, what makes the game much more tactical than Pachisi.

With 4 players it is a partner game and your partner´s pieces can be moved with your own cards.

The game is set in the children's fantasy world of Donner Knispel.

Home Page: http://www.myfleat.com/

Twenty clue slides are randomly placed in the slots on the gameboard. One of the eight criminal portraits is chosen secretly and placed inside the gameboard. The portrait is now hidden by the clue slides.

Each player, in turn, spins the spinner. The spinner will tell the player which two clues may be removed from the gameboard. The player may then guess which of the eight portraits is concealed by the slides by pressing the appropriate button on the gameboard. If the word "guilty" lights up, then the player has solved the mystery and won the game.

The game uses one "D" battery and a standard flashlight's bulb.

From the Box:

A new intergenerational board game for family and friends.  A game of luck, chance and strategy.  Can be played in combinations of two, four or eight players.  The object of the game is to fill your chosen colored areas, and to collect opponent's Jabongs.

Contains two Jagabongo boards, 64 Colored Jabongs and 3 Jates, one rule card and one bag.

Age 7+

Swedish trivia game about animals (The Fantastic World of Animals). On their turn, players choose their own task for that turn by playing a card from their hand. The categories are charade, picture quiz or one of five different categories of questions, e.g. “Animals in the air”. There are also wild cards. If the player succeeds in her task, she discards her task card. The first player to loose all her cards is the winner. When playing a charade card, the player has one minute to perform the charade. If another player makes the correct guess, then that player may discard any one card from her hand. The player performing the charade may also discard a card because the charade was a success.

8 years and up

Children´s game featuring Tummen (The Thumb), the character from the books of Inger Sandberg. Players roll the die and pick pieces from the box. Pieces have different colors on either end, e.g. yellow/blue pieces, red/green pieces. Each player is trying to make the longest row by connecting ends of the same color. If the die shows the Tummen side the player make take any piece, even from an opponent. This is where the puppets are used: The puppet is put on the player´s thumb and pointed at the piece the player wants. Longest row of pieces wins.

Contents:
49 puzzle pieces
Die w/ labels
4 thumb puppets
Rules

4 years and up
Game about the Mille's Sicilian campaign, the first campaign of Garibaldi against the Regno delle Due Sicilie. The game has 1 map, 107 counters and rules.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: Travel Time is a car themed spin-and-move game given as a toy with meal by Ruby's Diners.  Players move their car down a twisty road, following the instructions on the spaces.  Completely random and similar to Snakes and Ladders, though thankfully shorter.

Swedish children´s game featuring the characters Pettson and his cat Findus. Players help them make a birthday cake and celebrate. They roll the die and collect chits featuring parts of the cake. If you get a chit with a muckla (the small  ever present creatures from the books) you must give away a birthday present to another player. The first player to have a complete cake with candles, and who has received 2 birthday gifts, is the winner.

Contents:

Gameboard
colored die
42 small chits
48 big chits
Cloth bag
Rules

Game released in honor of the Dutch movie Abeltje (personal name of an elevator boy). On its turn the movie was based on a children's book by a famous Dutch writer: Annie M.G. Schmidt.

The game itself is a simple roll-and-move system, where some spots have a special meaning, sometimes requiring trivia questions about the movie.

Land on an elevator to move one floor up. Reach the top floor to win the game.

Yet another very simple TdF game, with a (more-or-less) 3D-board. No date provided, but must be somewhere in the sixties (after the Dutch Jan Janssen won the Tour in '68?). Distributed by a large Dutch warehouse chain.

Use the spinner to determine the number of spots to move forward. Landing on 20 is (for some reason) mandatory to proceed.

This game was released in the Netherlands by Hema and in Germany by Multiplay.

From the back of the game:

For more than a thousand years Kama Sutra has been a guide for lovers of all ages. We have interpreted this great work and offer it to a couple as an interactive game experience.

The Kama Sutra Game™ offers lovers the opportunity to explore the sensual and sexual aspects of their body, mind, and spirit on the journey to experiencing ecstasy.

Object of the Game
- To have fun developing a deeper understanding of what you and your lover like and want in your intimate relationship.

- To engage in an extended session of lovemaking that builds on anticipation, creativity, adventure, and a progression of physical intimacy.

- To expand your sensory awareness through activities using sight, hearing, taste, smell, and... touch.

Game Play
Rolling the die and moving your game pieces, each of you will travel a different path. During the game, the two of you will be communicating, sharing, and participating in a multitude of pleasurable activities and accumulating "Sexual Position" cards. These cards will be played to complete your "journey to ecstasy."

Included in the game:
A playing board, 56 activity and position cards, a die, 2 playing pieces, and instructions.

A Journey to Ecstasy awaits you!

Themed on a Swedish TV-quiz for school kids, this game pits 2-4 players or teams against each other. Players answer questions and perform tricky tasks with a time limit. Score markers are moved on a track, and some spaces on the track will trigger a die roll that decides whether players score positive or negative points. This adds a considerable random element.

Contents:
Scoring track
4 pawns
4 White boards
4 pencils and erasers
330 cards
10 topic cards
10 topic markers
Die
Timer
4 puzzles
1 plastic stand

10 years and up

The name Svensson is Sweden´s equivalent to Smith, and the title means Svensson asks, Svenssons answers. In this game players earn points by answering trivia questions but also by correctly guessing how many Swedes (in %) have correctly answered the same question on www.vetgirig.nu. People have also submitted questions for the game on the website.

Contents:
Gameboard
3 decks of cards
4 pawns
1 notepad
Rules

14 years and up

Pilare is a board game designed by the Basque game author Jorge Gómez Arrausi. It won a Proxime Accessit award in the Concurs de Creació de Jocs de Tona, 2005 ("Games Creation Contest of Tona").

Pilare is a stacking game in which pieces are moved in a way that resembles the sowing in Kalah games.

Swedish children´s game featuring spöket Laban (Little ghost Godfrey) from the books by Inger and Lasse Sandberg. The friendly ghost must practice some ghostly sounds. Players move around the board, representing the castle, and collect and imitate various sounds. The winner is the player who is the first to reach the Ghost Room and make the Great Ghostly Bawl.

Contents:
26 sound markers with drawings of cats, owls, cars etc.
4 key markers
4 pawns with plastic stands
Die
Rules

The Fall of Bataan is a two-player game of the American defense of the Bataan Peninsula against the Japanese in 1942. The mapsheet shows the major portion of the Bataan peninsula and the island of Corregidor. The game lasts 10 turns; at the end victory is evaluated based on the number of American units left on the map.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Goal of this game is to become the richest and the Ribaud with most influence in the city. "Ribaud" is an ancient French word which describes a person who likes luxury and extravagance.
You lead a team of crooks which you assign missions to. Each round consists of about 10 phases:
- Draw of a Season Card
- Liberation or execution of arrested team members
- Purchase of Event cards
- Announcement of a "big coup" and negotiation between players
- Secret placement of playing pieces
- Intervention of the police
- Taking back of playing pieces
- Solution of the "big coups"
- Income of players
- Occupation of streets  (and new turn)

After 8 rounds the grand winner is determined.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
From the promotional packaging inside of Zitadelle, viewable in Image 138790.

We need bread, meat, salt, fruit, dishes, and many more things for our household.  We can find these wares in five stores, or we can buy them in the countryside.  A new game that combines a lotto game with the ideals of a dice game.

From the promotional packaging inside of Zitadelle, viewable in Image 138790.

An interesting and varied board game.  Like in the game Halma, players must get their pieces into an opposite field.  However, the way to get there is over a turntable that one is allowed to turn one quarter, so long as there is another piece in the goal.  That is how the fortune of the party will most change.

Game rules:

This game can be played with up to 4 players.
Each player gets 10 meeples, of which 6 are 
to be moved into the opposite destination spaces 
of the same color.

The starting player is determined by chance.
The meeples can jump over other meeples of the same 
or any other color to get to the destination 
spaces (jumping is done like in the Halma game).

Every time a player gets one of his meeples on a 
destination space, the turntable gets turned a quarter
turn to the right.

This provides for variety in the gameplay, because 
every turn changes the position of the meeples 
for every player.

The winner is the player, who is the first to get 6
meeples on the destination spaces.

The 4 remaining meeples are without importance for the
outcome of the game.


Note: This game is played without a die.

A bilingual word game that uses a game board representing various rooms in a middle school.

The goal is to be the first player to complete proper Spanish sentences in each of eight rooms.

The objectives of this board game are twofold:

1. To teach bilingual students proper Spanish sentence structure.

2. The game also introduces students to the challenges they will face upon entering middle school.

The Scarney Dice set includes 5 special Scarney dice(each d6 contains a 1,3,4,6 and "dead" on 2 opposite sides), a dice cup, chips and 80 page rule book describing more than 40 different dice games that may be played with these dice.

Some of the games include:

Scarney Put and Take Dice
Scarney Duplicate Jackpots
Scarney 21 Up and Down

boardgame: Poor Bloody Infantry: Company Level Combat in WW2
description:
Poor Bloody Infantry 2006 is an updated version of the previous game PBI II: 15mm Scale Company Actions of World War 2 by the Rules For The Common Man team.

A miniatures game of World War 2 company level combat, PBI is designed to allow fast playing games that finish in about an hour. PBI uses a terrain map made from grid squares to address the old miniatures game problems of micro measuring and determining angles and line of sight.
Army lists are provided that span all the major countries involved and several of the minor ones over the course of the war.

PBI uses a new recon system that is substantially different than the old one, and much faster to apply. Other changes have streamlined gameplay or cleaned up rules that were somewhat confusing in the previous edition.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Series: Poor Bloody Infantry – WWII Miniature Rules (Peter Pig)

description: This is a supplement to the AK47 Republic miniature wargames rules providing extensions to the standard rules (cavalry, off table artillery, ambush, collateral, victory conditions, APCs, unarmed militia, night actions, minefields, boats and AA) along with rules for using AK47 for the war in Lebanon and Africa in the 1990s.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Magnetic based board game from the makers of Magz magnetic construction sets.

Each player has a set of Magnetic X tiles. Each tile has different symbols on its sides (hearts, spades, clubs and diamonds).  The timer is started and players race to add tiles to the board, making sure the symbols match.  Tiles can be added either 2 dimension or in 3D.

Scar-Nee and i-Q Solitaire (styled that way on the board) are two distinct abstract strategy games for one to four players, marketed together and using identical equipment.  Similar to Teeko but played on a 4x4 board and using 16 discs (4 different colors each numbered with pips 1 through 4).
It is likely that Scar-Nee is the same game or a direct forerunner of Scarney, and quite possible that i-Q Solitaire is also an ancestor of the solitaire versions of Scarney described in the latter's rule book.
Chess variant for 2 players (using a bigger board), 3 players (2 vs. 1) or 4 players (teams or individual)

The object  is to be the first player to unlock the Just Say No! Castle with a golden key. Players earn keys by answering a question on a card. There are no right or wrong answers. For example, "How would you say No if someone tried to give you a pill? Or "What does crack look like?" 
Game was endorsed by the National Just Say No! Foundation, a Nancy Reagan thing.

The goal of this game is to build a pyramid of monkeys. There are three types of monkeys and all three types come in three colors. Per turn you can only build your pyramid in one color. When building the other players only see the back of each monkey, so they don't know the constitution of other players' pyramids. The only way to find out is to attack! And attacking is also necessary to keep other players from finishing their pyramids. Finishing your pyramid is a true feat of bananalympics.

Designed and published by the sailboarding community in 1988, Pro-Am pits players (sailboarders) against each other as they race around the board collecting sponsorships, forging a career, and testing their knowledge on sailboarding. The game can be played on three different levels, from the experienced sail boarder down to the first timers and non-sailboarders who play on the 'Learner' level. Nicely outfitted with plenty of sponsorship options, career directions, and 'sailboards' pieces, the game delves deep into the world of sailboard racing.

Simple spin-and-move game distinguished only by presentation. The pawns are lead figures designed to resemble television cameras. The backdrop to the gameboard has pop-up figures giving the gameboard a 3-D effect. The game comes with a xylophone that must be played or the participant who misses the cue is penalized. The first to reach the finish space is the winner.

An unusual place on the GameBoard is the Station Break Area which is represented by clock symbols. If you land on or pass over one of these Station Breaks you must ring the TIME SIGNAL TONES to properly identify the network. If you fail to do so you must move your camera back 15 spaces.

Each player will act as quizmaster for another player so everyone can compete.  A random category for each round is determined by the Bid Wheel. Every correct answer earns the player fifty dollars. A round consists of four questions for each player. The winner of round chooses a card which represents a treasure chest and its treasure. The player also spins the Bid Wheel. The player may now choose between the known amount on the Bid Wheel (range: $250 to $2000) or take whatever happens to be on the card (range: $0 to $25,000)

The player who chooses and keeps the $25,000 treasure chest card is the immediate winner. Otherwise, the game goes the full four rounds. The winner is the person who has the most money after four rounds of play.

The game is based on a popular television series of the fifties.

The players take on the role of television show producer vying to book the best guests for television’s “The Today Show”. The deck is divided into four suits. Each suit represents a category of guest. Each suit has cards valued one through twelve; one being the greatest value and twelve having the least value. There are also four wild cards, valued at thirteen, to bring the total number of cards in the deck to fifty-two.

All players place their pawns on the start space. Each player chooses a card secretly. All players reveal their chosen cards simultaneously. The player who played the highest valued card gets to advance one’s own pawn the number of spaces equal to value of the winning card. The rules do not state what happens if two or more players tie. If two or more players play wild cards, there is no winner and the hand is replayed.

Certain spaces on the track assign a player who happens to land on them a personal trump suit. This trump stays in effect until the player moves on.

The hand is over when someone lands on the “Off the Air” space at the end of the track. The value of the cards remaining in the hand of the players is totaled and added to the score of the player who ended the round. All players receive the value of the cards in tricks each has taken. The first player to score five hundred points is the winner.

From the back of the rule book:

FUTURE TANK

Future tank is a two-player board game pitting players against each other with tank armies of their own choosing!

Players have four different types of tanks to choose from in which to build their armies:

1) Fast-Assault Tanks: Quick, highly maneuverable and able to outflank any other tank with cutting precision.

2) Light-Assault Tanks: Fast, but more heavily armored and more heavily weaponed. It can pack a solid punch.

3) Heavy-Assault Tanks: Slow, but carry the biggest gun!

4) Missile Tanks: Slow and heavily armored. Able to kill from a distance.

Rules are easy-to-read and play-action can let you start blasting away in the first turn! Terrain features are optional and six scenarios are included.

Average Play Time: 20-40 Minutes."

Played on a chess board, this games uses either the photocopied "counters" from the book, or minis of your choosing. You will also need a D4, D6, D8 and D12. A basic move and attack format. Lighter moves further and heaver shoots further. Each tank has a diagram showing its armor amounts (think SFB shields) and has ratings for movement, firing ranges and weapon damage.
UnStack was created by Professor João Pedro Neto, Lisbon (Portugal), in 2001. It is a stacking game in which counters are distributed in a way that resembles the "sowing" in Kalah games.

THE $64,000 QUESTION is a board game based on the UK version of the TV Game Show.  The aim of the game is to work along a question path answering questions and enter 'the isolator' to answer the $64,000 question.  The first player to get the $64,000 question correct is the winner.

Questions are categorized into 10 subject areas which are selected by the roll of a 10 sided die.  An additional category is provided when playing with younger players.

From the box:

"POETIC JUSTICE" is the super-duper, pretty-witty game where one word leads to another.  All you need is a dash of logic and a flash of inspiration to uncover the poetic solutions.

Poetic Cards consist of cleverly worked clues which are solved with rhyming couplets and a splash of humor!

Depending on where you land on the board you may have to play solo, challenge another player or join everyone else in this game of reason and rhyme."

NOEL'S HOUSE PARTY is a board game spin-off from the UK's TV Show of the same name.  It involves recreations of the main features of the show.

From the box:

"Round and around Crinkley Bottom you go... in for the Music Game .... down the cellar for 'Wait till I get you home'.  But who's that at the door? - better go and find out, oh no! you've landed in the grunge tank - or worse still you've to face the NTV talent test.

This new and exiting family game is fast-paced and action packed and features a fully working three-dimensional 'Grad a Grand' booth complete with miniature money".

The aim of the game is to collect the most money by moving around the board and performing a variety of actions based on the draw of a card:

NTV - act out the instructions on the card.
KNOCK KNOCK - Guess the identity of someone from clues on the card.
WAIT TILL I GET YOU HOME! - Guessing what answer another player will give to a question.
MUSIC GAME - Music based trivia or performance card.

Other spaces require you to spin again, miss a turn or take possion of the Gotcha! (allowing you to score double points whilst you have it).

Purple Ronnie's Groovy Game of RISKS AND RHYMES is an adult game involving moving around a board, making up poems, acting and drinking (preferably alcoholic beverages)

There are two decks of cards:
the first represents different character types (i.e.slob, sex goddess, mummy's boy ...)
the second comprises risks and rhymes.  Risk cards involve acting out whatever is written on the card.  The rhyme cards contain a short poem to which the final line needs to be added.

Players move round the board as per the roll of a die.  The space they land on determines what action they need to perform:
- Borrow a character card from another player
- Miss a turn
- take a Risk and Rhymes card for themselves or another player
- Roll again
- Give a character card to another player

All risks and rhymes must be performed in the style of the character card in play at the time with a successful performance resulting in winning of that character card.  The winner is the player who, at the end of the game, has collected the most character cards.

The players must deduce which of the six given historical figures is the correct one. The players choose and ask the questions on the cards given to them while the quizmaster responds according to the answer key. The player who gives the correct answer scores points depending on how many questions were asked by the players. The first to score one hundred points is the winner.

The game is loosely based on a television series designed by Monty Hall of Let’s Make a Deal fame. Mr. Hall’s name is misspelled on the box lid.

Four "snakes" consisting of 12 tokens each try to conquer numbered spaces on the game board. A dice rolling game for up to four players.

Try to surround a circle with your snake and score by placing a snake piece inside the surrounded circle. So your snake becomes shorter and you will have less pieces to surround the next circles.
A fishing game with semi-precious stones. Players hide  magnetic stones in 12 heaps of non-magnetic stones, then try to fish out as many magnetic stones from all heaps as they can.

Welcome to the jungle, baby! Now you're gonna die!

You’re going to war. Weird War.

boardgame: Joseph and His Coat of Many Colors
description:
Joseph And His Coat Of Many Colors is a variation on the popular game of UNO with uniquely illustrated cards depicting the biblical character of Joseph wearing his colored coats.  Players are trying to empty their hands of cards by playing them to a common pile, matching cards by color, number, or action.  The deck is made up of 4 sets of differently colored cards with numbers from 1 to 6.  There are also Miracle cards (wild cards), cards that cause the next player to pick up 1 or 2 cards, and Skip cards.

Note: This game is different from UNO in the following ways:

a) The map includes part of Southern Siam (Thailand).
b) A Siam Pass is up for auction after the Private Companies Auction but before the Stock buying round (on the first turn). Siam Pass is then auctioned out again when the first '3' train and '5' train is bought. The holder of the Siam Pass allow one of his/her Corporation to run trains into Siam without rolling the 'Train Hijack' die. (See Below) 
c) Train Hijack mechanics. Where, while running trains into Siam, the corporation president must roll a die. On the roll of 4,5 or 6, the train is hijacked and no revenues are generated from that train. The train card is then flipped over and cannot be used until after the next stock round.

DISASTER! is for ages 8 and up, 2 to 4 players with an average playing time 15 - 30 minutes.
Learn about natural disasters and how they affect the U.S, including how scientists rank the strengths of the different disasters. boardgame: Confucianism and the Succession Crisis of the Wanli Emperor, 1587
description:
From Description:

Confucianism and the Succession Crisis of the Wanli Emperor, 1587 seeks to introduce undergraduate students to the suppleness and power of Confucian thought as applied to issues of governance during the Ming dynasty.  The game is set in the Hanlin Academy.  Most students are members of the Grand Secretariat of the Hanlin Academy, the body of top-ranking graduates of the civil service examination who serve as advisers to the Wanli emperor.  Some Grand Secretaries are Confucian "purists,” who hold that tradition obliges the emperor to name his first-born son as successor; others, in support of the most senior of the Grand Secretaries, maintain that it is within the emperor’s right to choose his successor; and still others, as they decide this matter among many issues confronting the empire, continue to scrutinize the teachings of Confucianism for guidance.  The game unfolds amidst the secrecy and intrigue within the walls of the Forbidden City, as scholars struggle to apply Confucian precepts to a dynasty in peril.

This was the last stand of the South Vietnamese Army against all odds (which is why it may become an ATO issue game). The ARVN 18th division armed with M-48 Tanks and Cluster bombs conduct themselves in a heroic manner, so much so, that it becomes noticed by the outside world media press. The battle involved large amounts of aircraft and tanks. The ARVN defiant stand stunned the three NVA Divisions assigned to take the town. The two sides clashed for 10 days-it became a battle of honor! This game is a much improved version of Perry's earlier 1982 game.

System is similar to Perry Moore's Nanawa: Verdun in the Chaco War, July 1933.

description:
Children´s trivia game about Pippi Longstocking and friends.

Each player receives eight tiles with pictures from the books. Players lay them on the table in front of them and memorize the order of the pictures. Then they turn their tiles face-down. Players are then ready to take turns rolling the die and moving on the board.

If they land on a red spot, the game master picks up a red card and reads a quote from a story. The text is associated with one of the pictures on the players´ tiles. The first player to turn up the right tile gets a freckle painted in her face - a make-up stick is included in the game! As you know, Pippi is proud of her freckles.

If they land on a yellow spot, they get one freckle.

If they land on a green spot, the player answers a question from a card. If correct, the player gets one gold coin.

The first player to land on one of the blue spots gets the coin that was placed their before game start. No new coins are added during the game.

The first player to reach the finish gets three coins. The winner is the player with most points (total of freckles and coins!).

Age 5 years and up. Children will need someone to read the cards.

boardgame: The Threshold of Democracy: Athens in 403 B.C.
description:
The Threshold of Democracy: Athens in 403 B.C. is one of the first games in the Reacting to the Past game series originally created by Mark Carnes, history professor at Barnard College/Columbia University.  These games are intended for undergraduate or graduate students led by an instructor (facilitator)

Participants are assigned roles with secret role sheets and secret victory objectives.  The object of the game is to achieve the stated objective for each player.

The game is set in Athens soon after the tyrants installed by Sparta were overthrown.  The game focuses upon the future of democracy in Athens.  Participants are given a variety of roles: moderate democrats, radical democrats, oligarchs, Socratics and more.

Participants need read and refer to Plato's Republic (not included) and excerpts (included) from Thucydides, Xenophon, and other primary sources.

The game requires substantial reading, writing, and verbal debate.

Awards:
The Reacting to the Past series won the 2004 Hesburgh Award for innovative undergraduate education using gaming.

The American Historical Association awarded the 2006 William Gilbert Award to Mark C. Carnes for the best article on teaching history--focusing upon the Reacting to the Past games.
family: Series: Reacting to the Past (UNC Press)

boardgame: Age of Steam Expansion: Mars – Global Surveyor
description:
This Age of Steam Expansion expands the game to Mars.

Age of Steam Expansion - Mars: Global Surveyor is an expansion for the basic game Age of Steam. The originality of this expansion is that the green discs (the Martians) can move around the planet, and birth with blue cubes. The map uses the same mechanic as Age of Steam on the Moon and allows edges connections.

Each Anno Domini set consists of 336 cards, with a description of a historical event on one side of the card, and the year (and sometimes specific date) in which it happened on the other.

Each player receives nine of these cards (or less, if you want the game to be shorter), and may only look at the descriptions. Players then put in turn a card on the table, *trying* to put theirs in chronological order. A player may, instead of adding a card, claim that the order in which the cards were put is incorrect. In this case all cards are turned over and the correct years revealed.
If the order was correct then the doubting player receives two cards and skips a turn. If the order was incorrect then the previous player, who accepted the order as correct, receives three cards. The first player that gets rid of all the cards wins.

Bluffing is as least as important as knowledge in this game. Most events are rather obscure or are hard to date exactly, so this ain’t no game for the nerds/geeks of this world, even they might think so, because it’s full of surprises and pure fun. Highly recommended, because Anno Domini is a blast to play, is a very enjoyable social event and you even learn something from it.

Unfortunately there is no English edition in existence, but this game could be a good way, to improve your already existing German skills or a very good reason, to learn German language in the first place.

The set "Österreich" contains historical events which are related to Austria. This relationship is not necessary obvious. Like all Anno Domini the Austria edition can be played as standalone or mixed with some or even all editions.

Part of the Anno Domini series
This is a special Version for the 250th birthday of the Austrian lottery.

Directly from the rules:

By offering cards for completion the players jointly create a path that takes them through various periods taken from the life of Vincent Van Gogh.
The first player to get rid of all his cards is the winner.

boardgame: Rousseau, Burke, and Revolution in France, 1791
description:
From Description:

Rousseau, Burke, and Revolution in France, 1791 plunges students into the intellectual, political, and ideological currents that surged through revolutionary Paris in the summer of 1791.  Students are leaders of major factions within the National Assembly (and in the streets outside) as it struggles to create a constitution amidst internal chaos and threats of foreign invasion.  Will the king retain power?  Will the priests of the Catholic Church obey the “general will” of the National Assembly or the dictates of the pope in Rome?  Do traditional institutions and values constitute restraints on freedom and individual dignity or are they its essential bulwarks?


Are slaves, women, and Jews entitled to the “rights of man”?  Is violence a legitimate means of changing society or of purging it of dangerous enemies?  In wrestling with these issues, students consult Jean-Jacques Rousseau’s Social Contract and Edmund Burke’s Reflections on the Revolution in France, among other texts.

This is an early tile-placement game. The equipment consists of 80 hexagonal wooden tiles - 10 house tiles for each player, 1 town hall tile, and 39 road tiles of various configurations.

Players play a road tile of their choice each turn in an effort to create empty spaces with exactly three neighbors, in which they can place their houses. The object is place all of one's house tiles and then the town hall tile.

A slightly altered version was published (probably in the 1960s) by Whitman, with cardboard tiles. That game is called ZIGZAGS.

description:
Spitting Fire is a pseudo-historical game of aerial combat, set during the turbulent years of World War II.

Between 1939 and 1945, nearly the entire globe became entangled in the most expansive and destructive conflict ever seen. Although responsible for unimaginable terror and tragedy, the war also gave birth to innovation and invention on an unprecedented scale.

With Spitting Fire, players can take control of the aircraft forged in this crucible of conflict, and battle each other in skies the world over! We should state at the outset that those who are aching for a historically-accurate, painstakingly-researched, richly-detailed simulation of World War II dogfights should probably look elsewhere. While such games can be exciting, they aren’t what we had in mind. However, if you want a fast-playing, fun game that gives a nod to history without getting bogged down in historical minutiae, then Spitting Fire is for you.

Spitting Fire is designed to be used with any scale from 1:700 to 1:72 (although larger scales will require a substantial playing area). The rulebook includes data cards for 25 of the most famous fighters from World War II, with others to be released soon!

boardgame: Defining a Nation: India on the Eve of Independence, 1945
description:
From description: Defining a Nation: India on the Eve of Independence,1945 is set at Simla, in the foothills of the Himalayas, where the British viceroy has invited leaders of various religious and political constituencies to work out the future of Britain’s largest colony.  Will the British transfer power to the Indian National Congress, which claims to speak for all Indians?  Or will a separate Muslim state—Pakistan—be carved out of India to be ruled by Muslims, as the Muslim League proposes?  And what will happen to the vulnerable minorities—such as the Sikhs and untouchables—or the hundreds of princely states?  As British authority wanes, smoldering tensions among Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs increasingly flare into violent riots that threaten to ignite all India.  Towering above it all is the frail but formidable figure of Gandhi, whom some revere as an apostle of non-violence and others regard as a conniving Hindu politician.  Students struggle to reconcile religious identity with nation building—perhaps the most intractable and important issue of the modern world.  Texts include the literature of Hindu revival (Chatterjee, Tagore and Tilak); the Koran and the literature of Islamic nationalism (Iqbal); and the writings of Ambedkar, Nehru, Jinnah, and Gandhi.

As in other Reacting to the Past games, participants are given secret role sheets with victory objectives.  Debate and negotiation are the major mechanics used in this game.
family: Series: Reacting to the Past (UNC Press)

boardgame: Henry VIII and the Reformation Parliament
description: From Description: Henry VIII and the Reformation Parliament transforms students into lords and commoners and members of the English parliament during the tumultuous years of 1529-1536.  Cardinal Wolsey has just been dismissed as lord chancellor for failing to obtain from the pope the divorce king Henry is seeking from Catherine of Aragon, his wife of twenty years.  Thomas More is named as Wolsey’s replacement.  More presides over a newly summoned parliament, which the king hopes will somehow find the legal means to annul his marriage to Catherine, thus allowing him to proceed with his plans to marry Anne Boleyn and have by her a male heir.  But will parliament find the means, and will it be satisfied with solving the king’s marital and dynastic problems?  There are some in parliament who wish to use the royal divorce, as well as the rising anti-clericalism in the land, to effect a split from Rome and a conversion of England from Catholicism to Protestantism.  Other members oppose the divorce, oppose making the king head of the English church, and, most of all, oppose this new, heretical creed filtering in from the continent.  More is their leader, for as long as he can survive.  Thomas Cromwell leads the king’s party.  One problem is that the king is ambivalent about the reform effort unleashed by his so called “great matter,” and so the conservatives are free to prosecute reformers as heretics, while the reformers are free to prosecute conservatives as traitors.  Conservatives are liable to this charge because their frustration at home tempts them to consider petitioning the king of Spain and Holy Roman Emperor to invade England on behalf of Catholic Europe.  The game reaches its dramatic climax around the trial of Anne Boleyn, staged as a grand contest between opposing parties, which parties actually are multiple and fluid.  All roles are individualized and most are historically based.  At issue is the clash of four contending ideas:  medieval Catholicism, Lutheranism, Renaissance humanism, and Machiavellian statecraft.  Students read works representative of all traditions.

The title and theme comes from the Swedish TV show, licensed from BBC. So far, the game has only been published in Sweden.

The idea of the show, which is also running in Germany and perhaps other countries, is to have celebrities learning to dance and compete against each other. In the game, players do the same thing, following instructions on the DVD. There are also 225 cards with 1275 trivia questions on dancing, music and movies.

3-5 players or teams, ages 12 and up.

In "The Balloon Chase" players are trying to find five balloons, one of each color, for their game boards.

3 years and up

The game is published in Sweden under license from Bar-David, but there doesn't seem to be any other edition of the game.

N.E.W. Nano Engineered War is a strategy war game that engages players to use nano-technology to simulate ancient creatures from heads, bodies and legs pattern cards. Nanite material is required to create the creatures and each player fights for their individual clan to gain all of the collection material to help their tribe survive. The background of the game is as follows:

The planet Xioc has endured major wars throughout decades of time. The life sustainable resources of the planet have returned to their raw element forms. Air, food, water and plant life is scarce, and only a few humans have survived this mass devastation. Nano-technologies, more specifically molecular sized robots called nanots, were invented prior to the apocalyptic destruction. These self replicating machines along with a special material called nanite is the only way that humans have been able to synthesize small life sustainable ecosystems across the planet. Nanite is found in only trace amounts on the planet and is considered extremely valuable.

Factions and tribes of people living in anarchy, fight for what meager nanite remains. War is no longer waged in taking the life of another human directly, but is decided through nano-engineered war battle arenas. A small amount of battle nanite is used with nanots and pattern cards to create virtual creatures that fight for their tribe. The prize is to win the precious collection nanite that will allow the tribe to continue living.

You are the leaders of your respective clans and are battling to keep your family alive! Good luck.

Each player has two dice to move around the board which has an Inside and Outside track.  A dice on the Inside track must be moved before one on the Outside track.  Dice on the Inside track move the number of spaces rolled and try to get back to the Outside track.  Dice on the Outside track have more interesting movement options -- (a) move the number rolled, (b) roll 2,3,4,5 and move to that space on the Outside track if it is empty, (c) match a number showing on an opponents dice and take it's place while moving it to the Inside track, (d) roll one less than a number showing on another dice and move to the space just in front of that dice, (e) roll one more than a a number showing on another dice and move to the space just behind that dice.  Any move that causes one to land on an opponent's dice, moves that dice to the Inside track. 
The game goal is to land one's dice on one's GOAL space on the Oustside track. Each time a player scores a goal, he moves the scoring cube one stage on the scoring track towards his High Stage.  The scoring track is in the shape of an X.  The four tips of the X are the High Stage for each player.  The center of the X is the Zero Stage.  If another player scores first, a second scoring player would move the scoring cube back to the Zero Stage.  From the Zero Stage, a player would win by scoring 3 consecutive goals.
There is also a short version of the game where the scoring cube is not used and the first player to 3 goals wins.

boardgame: Stormbreaker Alex Rider: The Board Game
description:
Become the hottest new 14 year old spy, Alex Rider, and be the first to complete your mission by hacking your opponents secret code.

Based on the popular book series about the teenage super-spy, this game has players navigating the game board by collecting gadgets and special skills in order to complete various challenges and defeat the enemy.

Collect your assets within the Safe and Mission zones, but be careful when you enter the Caution, Heightened Alert and Danger zones your life could be in jeopardy!
